The current raw XML carries a materially more permissive public-domain availability statement than the legacy statement embedded in the pinned artifact; rights records preserve both claims separately.

rights.basis: The current raw XML says the work has been identified as public domain, free of known copyright restrictions including related and neighboring rights, and may be copied, modified, distributed, and performed commercially without permission. rights.component: Current public U-M/Medictionary raw XML, encoded Middle English text, and markup
notes: The United States jurisdiction follows the collection-level rights statement. The item's broader wording is preserved in evidence but not generalized here into a worldwide legal conclusion.
The current raw XML and pinned artifact preserve the same normalized alphanumeric FRONT and BODY text but are not identical XML serializations.

entities.description: A 1997 electronic text keyed by Sencor from Powell's 1908 edition, sampled at five percent and reported within one error per 20,000 characters, encoded by Catherine Paul, and markup-reviewed by Kenneth Church. It retains Powell's title page, Introductory Note, contents, medieval Attende lector, and work text through page 324; omits the edition's pages 325-330 word-list and nearly all manuscript marginalia; supplies missing day headings; and restructures chapters 3 and 33.
entities.label: U-M Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse electronic text LoveMirrour
notes: The current 644,118-byte public raw file is an ETS/EEBO serialization with updated availability wording; the pinned 632,979-byte artifact is DLPSTEXTCLASS. They are not byte- or canonical-XML-identical, but FRONT and BODY alphanumeric text hashes match after stripping whitespace, punctuation, symbols, and markup, and their BODY structures share 9 DIV1, 63 DIV2, 5 DIV3, 384 P, and 327 PB elements.

The current serialization converts the title page to a fourth FRONT DIV1, removes FOREIGN wrappers, changes page-break and note markup, and adjusts whitespace and punctuation boundaries while preserving the textual character sequence under the stated normalization.

rights.basis: The XML availability statement permits individual copying for personal use, research, and teaching with retention of the statement and permits linking, but expressly forbids redistribution by commercial or nonprofit content providers. It does not state a copyright license or public-domain status.
rights.component: Legacy U-M electronic transcription and markup as represented by the pinned DLPSTEXTCLASS artifact and its embedded 1997 availability statement
notes: The current public raw XML carries a later, materially more permissive public-domain statement. This legacy record preserves the terms actually embedded in the pinned artifact rather than silently replacing them.

bibliographic.citation: Nicholas Love, The Mirrour of the Blessed Lyf of Jesu Christ: A Translation of the Latin Work Entitled Meditationes Vitae Christi, Attributed to Cardinal Bonaventura, Made before the Year 1410, ed. Lawrence F. Powell (Oxford: Clarendon Press; London: Henry Frowde, 1908), iv, 330 pp.
bibliographic.title: The Mirrour of the Blessed Lyf of Jesu Christ

entities.description: The immediate print source of the U-M encoding. Powell prints the complete Brasenose beta-1 version, including its Attende lector, Love text, Eucharistic treatise, and final prayer, from Brasenose MS 9 with collation against Bodleian MS e. Musaeo 35 and the Sherard manuscript now Beinecke MS 324. Powell's editorial note, contents, and word-list are separate modern edition matter.
entities.label: Lawrence F. Powell's Mirrour of the Blessed Lyf of Jesu Christ (1908)

rights.basis: The underlying Middle English and Latin works were created in the Middle Ages and their copyright terms have expired.
rights.component: Nicholas Love's medieval Middle English text and the anonymous Latin source passages as represented in the 1908 edition
notes: This status does not transfer to U-M's encoding, modern editorial contributions, scan derivatives, or provider contract terms.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1908, before the current United States public-domain cutoff of 1 January 1931 stated by the Copyright Office for works as of 2026.
rights.component: Powell's 1908 editorial note, punctuation, collation, word-list, and other original editorial contributions

rights.basis: UK guidance states that written works usually last for the author's life plus 70 years. Powell died in 1975, so original contributions may remain protected through 2045, subject to facts and exceptions not established here.
rights.component: Powell's original literary and editorial contributions, excluding the expired typographical arrangement
notes: This is a cautious status, not a determination of ownership or enforceability.

rights.basis: UK guidance gives typographical arrangement of published editions a 25-year term; the 1908 arrangement has expired.
rights.component: Typographical arrangement of the 1908 published edition

access.accessMethod: No complete public facsimile was located in targeted institutional and scholarly searches; enquire with Brasenose for reproductions.

entities.description: Powell's base witness and Sargent's beta-1 witness Bc, produced in the early fifteenth century on parchment by one scribe. The Mirror includes a table, prohemium beginning at fol. 5r, complete beta-1 text through the explicit and Love colophon at fol. 150v, and a fragmentary Middle English Meditationes de Passione whose leaves were removed and final passage crossed out. That adjacent item is not part of the cataloged Mirror.

holding.notes: Brasenose's current pages say most manuscripts are kept and consulted at the Bodleian, but the exact present request route and custody arrangement for MS 9 were not established from an official item record.

entities.label: Oxford, Brasenose College MS 9

physicalDescription.extent: ii + 149 + i leaves; flyleaves numbered 1-151, with an unnumbered leaf between 105 and 106
notes: The table is present by fol. 4r, the prohemium begins fol. 5r, and the Mirror explicit and Love colophon are on fol. 150v.

The Geographies description assigns the one scribe the project's Delta designation and a western Buckinghamshire dialect near the Oxfordshire border; this is witness evidence, not a precise place of Love's composition.

rights.basis: The underlying witness text was produced in the early fifteenth century and its copyright term has expired.
rights.component: Medieval textual content of the Mirror witness

rights.basis: No exact public reproduction terms were established for this witness; consultation with the current custodian is required.

dateStatements.label: Mirror section production
dateStatements.value: About 1420; late first quarter of the fifteenth century

entities.description: One of Powell's two collation witnesses and Sargent's alpha-1 witness Mu. The composite parchment manuscript contains the Mirror at pages 1-218, followed by a prayer at pages 219-220 and The Mirror to Lewed Men and Women at pages 221-467. Those adjacent items are outside the cataloged work. Current scholarship dates the Mirror section to about 1420 or the late first quarter of the fifteenth century and associates its production with Margaret Neville and Thomas Beaufort.

holding.notes: The public scholarly description derives from microfilm and earlier cataloging. The current Bodleian request and copying pages provide the verified access routes.

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library MS e. Musaeo 35, item 1

physicalDescription.extent: Composite manuscript, vii + 470 pages; the Mirror occupies pages 1-218
notes: The Mirror text is copied by the description's scribe A through page 218.

access.accessMethod: No complete public image set was found in the official catalog or targeted public searches; order reproductions or consult onsite.
access.entityLabel: New Haven, Yale University, Beinecke Library MS 324

dateStatements.label: manuscript production
dateStatements.value: Second quarter of the fifteenth century; probably London, circa 1430-1450

entities.description: Powell's second collation witness, explicitly identified by the official Beinecke catalog as the Sherard manuscript formerly in Lord Aldenham's collection. Its Mirror table occupies fols. v-vi and text fols. 1r-126v, followed on fol. 126v by the Arundel Memorandum. Several folios are missing, and the scribe deliberately omits Powell pages 308-324; the witness is therefore incomplete relative to Powell's Brasenose text.

holding.notes: The official catalog traces ownership from Nicholas Ferrar through James Sherard, Henry Wilson, Lord Aldenham, George Smith, and C. A. Stonehill before Beinecke purchase in 1965.

entities.label: New Haven, Yale University, Beinecke Library MS 324

physicalDescription.extent: iii + i + 128 + iii leaves; Mirror table fols. v-vi and text fols. 1r-126v
notes: Missing leaves correspond to Powell pages 7-9, 11-20, 214-217, 236-238, and 299-302; Powell pages 308-324 were deliberately omitted.

rights.basis: The underlying witness text was produced in the fifteenth century and its copyright term has expired.
rights.component: Medieval textual content of the Mirror witness and Arundel Memorandum

rights.basis: Beinecke states that a reproduction order does not imply publication authorization, that publication images should be ordered through its Digital Studio, and that the library generally does not hold copyright in collection content. Exact terms depend on the requested images and use.
rights.component: Any future Beinecke digital reproductions

bibliographic.citation: Meditationes vitae Christi, anonymous pseudo-Bonaventuran Latin devotional work, traditionally attributed to Bonaventure.
bibliographic.title: Meditationes vitae Christi

entities.description: The abstract Latin devotional source work from which Love selected, condensed, reordered, translated, and supplemented material. Medieval and Powell framing attributes it to Bonaventure; current scholarship treats it as pseudo-Bonaventuran or anonymous. This entity does not assert use of a particular surviving Latin manuscript or authorship by John of Caulibus.
entities.label: Meditationes vitae Christi (abstract Latin source work)

rights.basis: The underlying Latin work was created in the Middle Ages and its copyright term has expired.
rights.component: Medieval Latin source work

entities.description: A privately printed collection catalog. Page 200 describes the Sherard manuscript that is now Beinecke MS 324. Page 21 describes a different Mirror manuscript, later Takamiya MS 63, whose supplied portions were copied from the Sherard manuscript; that separate witness is not one of Powell's three and is not added to the direct lineage graph.

Ask whether consultation and reproduction requests should be placed directly through the Bodleian and request an official exact-item record if one exists.
notes: Powell's 1908 statement that the manuscript was deposited at the Bodleian is historical evidence, not confirmation of today's operational route.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact current custody and request path for Brasenose College MS 9?

next_steps: Use the recorded consultation and reproduction-order routes if full page images are required for passage-level work.
notes: The archived Bodleian page-80 JPEG is explicitly partial and must not be described as a complete facsimile.
openQuestions.question: Can complete public facsimiles be established for Brasenose MS 9, Bodleian MS e. Musaeo 35, or Beinecke MS 324?

next_steps: Map Powell's notes and variants against the three witnesses or Sargent's critical apparatus before assigning passage-level ancestry.
Keep the present collation relations at selected-passage scope until that work is complete.
openQuestions.question: Which exact Powell readings derive from Bodleian MS e. Musaeo 35 and Beinecke MS 324?

next_steps: Evaluate the proposed Ripon Cathedral MS 6 connection and reading-level comparisons in current scholarship.
Retain only the abstract Latin source-work relation until physical use is established.
openQuestions.question: Which physical Latin Meditationes vitae Christi exemplar or exemplars did Nicholas Love use?

next_steps: Ask U-M whether the current public-domain assertion supersedes the legacy availability statement for earlier DLPSTEXTCLASS serializations as well as the current ETS/EEBO file.
Ask the CME repository maintainers whether the Unlicense was intended to cover inherited XML data, and preserve the embedded legacy statement meanwhile.
openQuestions.question: How do the legacy U-M redistribution restrictions, current U-M public-domain assertion, and CME Unlicense apply to the exact pinned General Corpus artifact?

next_steps: Confirm any publication agreements, assignments, and applicable UK transitional provisions.
Treat original Powell contributions as potentially protected in the UK through 2045 unless qualified advice establishes otherwise.
notes: The typographical arrangement has a separate expired term and is not the unresolved component.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact UK copyright and ownership status of Powell's original 1908 editorial contributions?

next_steps: Consult Sargent's full critical introduction and apparatus before assigning individual additions or the Eucharistic treatise to a precise revision event.
Continue qualifying Powell's Brasenose text as beta-1 rather than a universal final text.
openQuestions.question: How should the chronology and exact contents of Love's beta and alpha authorial forms be dated and related at passage level?

reviewNotes: Completeness refers to the Powell/Brasenose beta-1 version, not to one universal final authorial form of a work transmitted in early beta and alpha revisions.

The Latin Attende lector printed on Powell page [6] and encoded at LoveMirrour:3 is medieval witness paratext; it is not Powell editorial prose even though it appears after his contents and inside XML FRONT.

Powell's 'MS. Brasenose College e. 9' is current Oxford, Brasenose College MS 9; his Sherard/Lord Aldenham collation witness is current Yale Beinecke MS 324, not the different Aldenham Mirror manuscript now Takamiya MS 63.
No complete public manuscript facsimile was verified for the three source witnesses. The only cached manuscript image is one archived sample of Bodleian MS e. Musaeo 35, page 80.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is the exact pinned General Corpus copy of a 1997 University of Michigan electronic text keyed from Lawrence F. Powell's 1908 edition. Powell printed the complete Brasenose MS 9 beta-1 version of Nicholas Love's Middle English Mirror, including the Latin Attende lector witness paratext, sixty-three chapters in seven day-sections, the integrated Eucharistic treatise conventionally numbered chapter 64, and final prayer; he based the text on Brasenose MS 9 and collated Bodleian MS e. Musaeo 35 and the Sherard manuscript now Yale Beinecke MS 324. U-M retained selected editorial front matter and the Powell/Brasenose text through page 324, omitted Powell's word-list and nearly all manuscript marginalia, and adjusted several headings and subdivisions. The graph keeps Love's adapted Middle English work, its abstract pseudo-Bonaventuran Latin source, physical witnesses, print edition, digital encoding, repository artifact, facsimiles, supporting catalogs, access routes, and rights layers distinct.

catalogSummary.author: Nicholas Love
catalogSummary.date: Translated and adapted before or around 1410
catalogSummary.region: England; exact place of composition or translation not securely established
catalogSummary.title: The Mirror of the Blessed Life of Jesus Christ

catalogingSubject.label: The Mirror of the Blessed Life of Jesus Christ
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The complete Powell/Brasenose beta-1 version of Nicholas Love's Middle English prose translation, adaptation, and compilation of material from the pseudo-Bonaventuran Meditationes vitae Christi: the Latin Attende lector witness paratext, prohemium, sixty-three chapters arranged for meditation across seven days, and the integrated Eucharistic treatise and final prayer conventionally numbered chapter 64. The cataloged work excludes Powell's 1908 title page, Introductory Note, table of contents, and word-list, while the linked lineage manifest records those edition and encoding boundaries. It is not a claim to one universal final authorial form, the Latin Meditationes itself, a generic life of Christ, or every item in the Brasenose manuscript.

contentParts.label: Attende lector
notes: Although encoded within FRONT and printed after Powell's contents, this is medieval witness paratext and belongs to the cataloged textual unit.
contentParts.summary: A Latin instruction to the reader explains the manuscript's marginal N and B letters as distinguishing Love's translator-compiler material from passages derived from the work attributed to Bonaventure.

contentParts.label: Prohemium
contentParts.summary: Love explains the work's use for simple devout readers, its imaginative method, its relation to the Latin Meditationes, and the recommended weekly and liturgical reading pattern.

contentParts.label: Monday: Incarnation and early life
notes: Chapter 3 has three encoded subparts; these do not increase the canonical chapter count.

Its final Ave Maria meditation contains Love's authorial rhyme, with the additional 'Gete us' lines characteristic of the beta-1 version. Powell and the XML run the passage as prose, so one verse group is grounded but a line count is not.
contentParts.summary: Nine chapters move from the heavenly counsel and Mary's life through the Incarnation, Annunciation, Visitation, Nativity, Circumcision, Epiphany, and Purification.

contentParts.label: Tuesday: Childhood and preparation for ministry
contentParts.summary: Five chapters treat the flight into and return from Egypt, Christ in the Temple, his hidden life, and his Baptism and withdrawal into the desert.

contentParts.label: Wednesday: Temptation, teaching, and early ministry
contentParts.summary: Ten chapters cover Christ's fast and temptations, gathering of disciples, Cana, the Sermon on the Mount, healings, Mary Magdalene's conversion, and controversies with Pharisees.

contentParts.label: Thursday: Later ministry and the Last Supper
notes: The encoding combines two chapter-33 headings as subparts of one chapter.
contentParts.summary: Fifteen chapters move through miracles, active and contemplative life, Lazarus, the entry into Jerusalem, and the Last Supper, ending at the threshold of the Passion.

contentParts.label: Friday: Passion and burial
contentParts.summary: Nine chapters guide compassionate meditation from Christ's prayer and arrest through trials, Crucifixion, death, deposition, burial, and the disciples' and Mary's grief.

contentParts.label: Saturday: Mourning and the descent into hell
contentParts.summary: One chapter depicts Mary, the apostles, and the women in mourning and preparation, then contemplates Christ's descent to the fathers in hell.

contentParts.label: Sunday: Resurrection, Ascension, and Pentecost
contentParts.summary: Fourteen chapters treat the Resurrection and appearances, the Ascension, and the descent of the Holy Spirit, closing the Speculum vitae Christi proper.

contentParts.label: A Short Treatise of the Sacrament of Christ's Blessed Body
notes: The table treats this integrated work as chapter 64; the XML gives it a top-level treatise division rather than a DIV2 chapter.
contentParts.summary: The treatise expounds the Real Presence, obedience to Church teaching, Eucharistic miracles, and anti-Lollard polemic, making the sacrament a memorial and culmination of Christ's life.

contentParts.label: Final Eucharistic prayer

locators.value: LoveMirrour:12, final two P elements; pp. 323-324

contentParts.summary: A prayer for devout recitation before the sacrament asks Christ for healing, grace, virtue, amendment of life, and final union with him.

assertion.notes: The terminal year follows the traditional presentation/approval chronology; scholarship cautions that the text circulated in more than one early authorial form.

notes: The normalized upper bound is not a claim that every revision or the transmitted Memorandum was completed in 1410.

extent.description: Sixty-four numbered items: sixty-two prose-only DIV2 chapters plus mixed chapter 3 are organized under seven day-sections, and the top-level prose Eucharistic treatise is conventionally numbered chapter 64, yielding 63 prose-only and one mixed numbered item. Eleven modeled content parts comprise the Latin Attende lector witness paratext, prohemium, seven days, treatise, and nested final prayer. Chapter 3 contains one brief authorial rhyme group, but Powell and the XML do not preserve lineation securely enough for a verse-line count; the XML's P-only encoding is not evidence that the rhyme is prose. Three and two DIV3 subdivisions respectively refine chapters 3 and 33 without increasing the chapter total. Counts exclude Powell's title page, Introductory Note, table of contents, and word-list.

formStatements.description: A predominantly prose devotional translation and adaptation. Chapter 3 ends with Love's brief rhyming Ave Maria meditation, including the extra beta-1 'Gete us' rhyme; this selected mixed passage is recorded at part and extent level without changing the whole-work discovery form.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The XML header's lone LANGUAGE value, Latin, is erroneous as a whole-work description.

scope.description: Rubrics, quotations, chapter apparatus, the Attende note, explicit, and colophon embedded within the predominantly Middle English work

notes: The transmitted Arundel Memorandum is historical evidence within the manuscript tradition, not treated here as a surviving administrative approval record or as proof that one final form was fixed in 1410.

The Brasenose scribe's western Buckinghamshire dialect localization and the other source witnesses' dates belong to their material transmission layers, not to the abstract work's composition place or date.

Powell's twenty-three-manuscript count and late-fifteenth-century dating of both collated witnesses are retained as historical edition claims but superseded for current description by later witness scholarship and institutional cataloging.

next_steps: Consult Sargent's full critical introduction and apparatus before assigning individual additions or the Eucharistic treatise to a precise revision event.
notes: The Brasenose-based Powell text represents beta-1, while the work survives in multiple early forms; a single 1410 completion event would overstate the evidence.
openQuestions.question: How should the chronology and exact contents of Love's early beta form and later alpha revision be dated at passage level?

next_steps: Evaluate the proposed Ripon Cathedral MS 6 connection and reading-level comparisons in current scholarship; retain only the abstract Meditationes source relation until physical use is established.
openQuestions.question: Which physical Latin exemplar or exemplars did Love actually use?

next_steps: Consult biographical and textual scholarship that distinguishes Love's Mount Grace office from evidence for where each textual phase was produced.
openQuestions.question: Can the place or places of translation, compilation, and revision be established more narrowly than England?

next_steps: Ask U-M for production metadata if the defect's history matters; continue faceting the inspected work as Middle English with embedded Latin.
openQuestions.question: Was the XML header's whole-work LANGUAGE value Latin introduced by conversion error, inherited metadata, or a misunderstood source-language field?

notes: Love's office at Mount Grace is an association, not sufficient evidence for an exact place of translation; the Brasenose scribe's dialect and manuscript production place are also separate witness-level claims.

Whole-work authorship describes Love's resulting Middle English work; the separate responsibilities below preserve his translator, adapter, and compiler functions and do not assign his source-derived passages to Pseudo-Bonaventure as authorship of this work.

scope.description: Authorship of the resulting Middle English Mirror as a translated, adapted, and compiled work
Middle English rendering of selected and reorganized material from the Meditationes vitae Christi
Selection, condensation, reordering, and expansion of the pseudo-Bonaventuran source, including anti-Lollard and Eucharistic material
Compilation of translated source matter, scriptural and patristic authorities, original connective material, and the Eucharistic treatise
The Brasenose-based 1908 source edition and its modern editorial framing, not authorship of the medieval work
Especially the Last Supper chapter and the concluding treatise and prayer
Love's orthodox polemic concerning confession, Eucharistic doctrine, and obedience to ecclesiastical teaching
The Annunciation and Nativity, Mary's compassion at the Passion, Holy Saturday, and the first Resurrection appearance

summaries.value: Nicholas Love's Middle English Mirror guides readers through imaginative meditation on Christ's life from the heavenly counsel before the Incarnation through Nativity, ministry, Passion, Resurrection, Ascension, and Pentecost. Recasting and condensing the pseudo-Bonaventuran Meditationes vitae Christi into sixty-three chapters for seven days of reading, Love adds extensive vernacular explanation, devotional direction, and orthodox polemic. A concluding Eucharistic treatise and prayer defend the Real Presence and make the sacrament the culminating memorial of Christ's earthly life.

The cataloged Powell/Brasenose beta-1 version includes the medieval Latin Attende lector witness paratext, prohemium, seven day-sections, sixty-three narrative and expository chapters, the Eucharistic treatise conventionally numbered chapter 64, and its final prayer. The U-M XML additionally retains Powell's title page, Introductory Note, and table of contents, but omits Powell's pp. 325-330 word-list and nearly all manuscript marginalia. The editorial front and encoding choices are not treated as medieval authorship, and completeness is not a claim to one universal final authorial form.

The XML's LANGUAGE value Latin is a header defect: the work is predominantly Middle English prose with embedded Latin. Powell's source is a beta-version text based on Brasenose College MS 9 and collated with two other witnesses; its spellings, punctuation, chapter structure, and omissions therefore describe an edited transmission layer rather than a single authorial original.

notes: Modern preferred form used by current scholarship; historical spelling is retained as an alternate title.
titles.value: The Mirror of the Blessed Life of Jesus Christ

notes: Powell's title-page form, normalized only by adding the initial article used on that title page.
titles.value: The Mirrour of the Blessed Lyf of Jesu Christ

notes: Latin title form preserved in the explicit and witness descriptions; it must not be confused with the language of the whole work.
